title: Born Three Months After Brown
summary: Glenda Funchess introduces herself, born in Hattiesburg, Mississippi on August 17, 1954, which she notes was three months after the Brown v. Board of Education decision. She comes from a family of twelve children, with her mother originally from Alabama and her father from Jefferson Davis County, Mississippi.

title: Her Parents and Twelve Siblings
summary: Funchess describes her mother's work as one of the first homemaker social-worker aides with the Department of Human Services and her father's long career as a chef at the University of Southern Mississippi. She was the sixth youngest of twelve, and her oldest sister had already joined the Great Migration to Cleveland, Ohio.

title: A Segregated Town and Freedom Summer
summary: Funchess recalls a segregated Hattiesburg with thriving Black businesses, cafes, cleaners, and hotels, and explains that she became politically aware at age nine during Freedom Summer, when SNCC students showed the community rights it was not exercising. She began school in a local activist's kindergarten before attending Mary Bethune Elementary.

title: The Stair-Step Desegregation Plan
summary: Funchess explains Hattiesburg's stair-step approach to dismantling the dual school system from 1965 through 1968, adding four grades each year until all twelve were desegregated. She was asked in the late 1960s to attend an affluent, distant white junior high that had enrolled only four or five Black students the prior year.

title: The NAACP Education Committee Asks
summary: A member of the NAACP education committee requested that Funchess and her sister, both strong students, transfer into the white junior high, and their mother agreed. Funchess recalls that her mother's employer, a paralyzed man whose family she nursed, demanded to know what the NAACP had given her to send her children there.

title: Expectation Versus Reality
summary: A neighbor who had transferred to the poor-white school the year before briefed the children daily, leading them to believe the affluent white school would be friendlier because Freedom Summer had taught them that poorer whites embraced the Klan. They assumed the wealthier students would be easier, only to find a harsh reality.

title: Nine Months Alone at Lunch
summary: Funchess describes a hostile school where no one spoke to her and students jumped up whenever she set her lunch tray down, isolation she attributes to the principal setting the tone. Years later a former classmate wrote from Delaware to apologize, having seen an article about Funchess after she dedicated a civil rights marker honoring Dr. King's 1968 visit.

title: Seated Alone, Her Paper Destroyed
summary: On her first day a science teacher moved Funchess to a solitary front table, where she sat the entire year. After she wrote a history paper on Dr. King, whom she had heard speak about the Poor People's Campaign at a local church weeks before his death, classmates cut her completed paper to pieces.

title: No Counseling for the Children
summary: Funchess recalls riding a neighbor's Volkswagen van with about fifteen children and argues the district failed them by providing no counseling amid resistance from the White Citizens' Council and the Sovereignty Commission. She recounts learning of an African American boy at the school who was struck in the face with his own shoes during her time there.

title: Choir Music and Racist Remarks
summary: Funchess loved choir but recalls a classmate crying because she did not want her parents to see her standing next to Funchess at a graduation program, after which a student was moved. She also remembers boys in choir using racial slurs when joking about singing Negro spirituals.

title: Separated, Then Leaving the School
summary: Funchess explains that she and the only other Black eighth grader were given identical schedules but placed in separate class periods, sharing only a twenty-minute homeroom. After a discomfiting nursing-home choir performance and morning football drills in which a girl's arm was broken, Funchess left the school because her grades were certain to drop.

title: A Better School and Calls at Home
summary: Funchess transferred to a poor-white school on the other side of town and credits its administrator with setting a welcoming tone, noting an African American girl co-hosted a program there. She received harassing phone calls at home from students at the affluent school, then returned to Rowan, the African American high school, after ninth grade.

title: Zoning Brings White Students to Rowan
summary: Funchess describes a zoning plan in her eleventh-grade year that sent a handful of white students across the railroad tracks to Rowan, the African American high school. A Black history program later recognized two white students, including the son of a local college president, who had enrolled at the Black school.

title: Consolidation and a Segregation Academy
summary: Funchess estimates the city was about thirty-five percent African American and recalls that most whites stayed when the two school systems consolidated in 1972 following the Alexander v. Holmes ruling. She notes that a local segregation academy founded in the mid-1960s offered some white families an alternative to the integrated public schools.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education
